masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through the 50 States
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through the 50 States
masthead.dek: A big round on the states — physical geography, people and economy, history, names, and symbols. Every answer is a state (or a count) reached by reasoning, not recall: the biggest city that isn't the capital, the wettest place that isn't where you'd guess. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing U.S. states questions. Verified to the Census Bureau, USGS, NPS, BEA, and Britannica.

dimensions: capital sharing no letters with state name
home states of the most-visited national park
states meeting at the Four Corners point
count of state names starting with a vowel
count of states with Native American-derived names
plan.note: Three branches (physical geography, population/economy, history/names/symbols), each answer a state or count, each with a reflex-defeater; fresh dimensions throughout.

questions.q: Two states sit below sea level at their lowest spot. One of them holds the lowest dry land in the whole country. Which state is that?
questions.detail: Badwater Basin in Death Valley sits 282 feet below sea level, the lowest point of land in North America.
questions.why: People reflex to a swampy coastal state like Florida or Louisiana, but the national low point is a desert basin inland in California.
questions.more: Louisiana is the other state with land below sea level (near New Orleans), but its low point is far shallower than Badwater Basin.
questions.source: Statista (lowest point by state); NPS Death Valley

questions.q: One state's lowest spot is still higher than the highest point in 18 other states. Which state never dips low?
questions.detail: Colorado's lowest point, on the Arikaree River, is about 3,315 feet up, the highest 'low point' of any state.
questions.why: Mountain states like Wyoming or Utah come to mind, but Colorado uniquely has no land below 3,000 feet.
questions.more: That 3,315-foot floor beats the summit of every Appalachian-and-eastward state's highest peak except a few.
questions.source: Denver Gazette (Arikaree River low point); USGS elevation data

questions.q: Leaving out Alaska, which state has the longest coastline of all?
questions.detail: Florida has about 8,400 miles of tidal shoreline, second only to Alaska nationwide.
questions.why: California feels like the obvious 'beach' answer, but Florida's peninsula plus its bays and keys give it far more shoreline.
questions.more: Alaska alone has more shoreline than all the other states combined, so it is set aside here.
questions.source: NOAA Office for Coastal Management shoreline mileage; WorldAtlas

questions.q: Which state has the rainiest yearly average of any of the lower 48?
questions.detail: Louisiana averages about 60 inches of precipitation a year, the most among the contiguous states.
questions.why: Hawaii is wetter overall but is excluded here; on the mainland, the warm Gulf moisture makes Louisiana the wettest.
questions.more: Mississippi and Alabama trail close behind, all soaked by Gulf of Mexico air.
questions.source: Current Results (average annual precipitation by state); WorldAtlas

questions.q: Which state has the driest yearly average of any in the country?
questions.detail: Nevada averages about 10 inches of precipitation a year, the only state under that mark.
questions.why: Arizona and New Mexico feel like deserts too, but the Sierra Nevada rain shadow leaves Nevada the driest statewide.
questions.more: The Sierra Nevada wrings out Pacific moisture before it reaches Nevada, and the Rockies block Gulf moisture.
questions.source: NOAA/NCICS Nevada State Climate Summary; The Nevada Independent fact brief

questions.q: One state has never once recorded a temperature below zero degrees Fahrenheit anywhere in it. Which state is that?
questions.detail: Hawaii's coldest reading ever is 12 degrees, atop Mauna Kea, and it has never gone below zero.
questions.why: Florida sounds warm enough to qualify, but Florida has dipped below zero in its panhandle; only Hawaii never has.
questions.more: Even Hawaii's high volcano summits stay above zero, thanks to the surrounding ocean.
questions.source: Wikipedia (U.S. temperature extremes); Plantmaps Hawaii records

questions.q: By share of its land, which state is the most blanketed in forest?
questions.detail: About 89 percent of Maine is forested, the highest share of any state.
questions.why: Pacific Northwest states feel foresty, but Maine's near-total tree cover tops them all by percentage.
questions.more: New Hampshire is next at roughly 83 percent, also a heavily wooded New England state.
questions.source: Visual Capitalist (most forested states); USDA Forest Service

questions.q: The longest river that flows from start to finish inside a single state is found in which state?
questions.detail: The Brazos River runs about 1,280 miles entirely within Texas, the longest river contained in one state.
questions.why: Big rivers like the Missouri or Mississippi cross many states; the longest that stays within one set of borders is in Texas.
questions.more: Alaska's longest in-state river, the Kuskokwim, is far shorter at about 700 miles.
questions.source: Wikipedia (longest rivers in the U.S. by state)

questions.q: Counting every state it flows along or through, how many states does the Mississippi River touch?
questions.detail: The Mississippi borders or runs through 10 states, from Minnesota down to Louisiana.
questions.why: People undercount by forgetting that for much of its length the river is a state line touching two states at once.
questions.more: The 10 are Minnesota, Wisconsin, Iowa, Illinois, Missouri, Kentucky, Arkansas, Tennessee, Mississippi, and Louisiana.
questions.source: Britannica (Mississippi River); American Cruise Lines

questions.q: Two states are tied for bordering the most other states. How many states does each of them touch?
questions.detail: Missouri and Tennessee each border eight other states, the most of any state.
questions.why: People guess a high number like 10 or pick just one winner, but two states tie at exactly eight.
questions.more: Missouri and Tennessee even border each other, and both reach eight neighbors.
questions.source: WorldAtlas (states bordering the most other states)

questions.q: The point that balances all 50 states like a flat map sits in which state?
questions.detail: The geographic center of all 50 states is about 20 miles north of Belle Fourche, South Dakota.
questions.why: People guess Kansas because it centers the lower 48, but adding Alaska and Hawaii in 1959 pulled the point northwest into South Dakota.
questions.more: Before 1959 the all-states center sat near Lebanon, Kansas; the new states shifted it north.
questions.source: Wikipedia (geographic center of the United States); CLUI

questions.q: Across all 50 states, how many actually touch the Pacific Ocean?
questions.detail: California, Oregon, Washington, Alaska, and Hawaii are the five states on the Pacific.
questions.why: People often forget Alaska and Hawaii or miscount the mainland trio, landing on three or four.
questions.more: Only the mainland West Coast trio is connected by land; Alaska and Hawaii round it to five.
questions.source: NOAA coastal states; U.S. Census coastline data

questions.q: Among states with a saltwater coast, which one has the shortest stretch of general coastline?
questions.detail: New Hampshire has only about 13 miles of general coastline, the least of any ocean state.
questions.why: Tiny states like Delaware or Rhode Island come to mind, but New Hampshire's narrow seaward gap is even shorter.
questions.more: That short coast sits between Maine and Massachusetts at the state's southeast corner.
questions.source: NOAA Office for Coastal Management coastline mileage

questions.q: Which state contains the headwaters where the Mississippi River actually begins?
questions.detail: The Mississippi starts at Lake Itasca in northern Minnesota, where you can step across it.
questions.why: People associate the great river with the Deep South and guess a Gulf state, but it is born far north in Minnesota.
questions.more: At Itasca the river is a shallow stream only a few feet wide.
questions.source: USGS; Britannica (Mississippi River)

questions.q: Among the lower 48, the largest natural lake that lies entirely inside one state, rather than on a border, is in which state?
questions.detail: The Great Salt Lake, historically near 1,700 square miles, is the largest natural lake fully within one of the contiguous states.
questions.why: The famous big lakes are the Great Lakes, but those are shared borders; the biggest wholly inside one lower-48 state is in Utah.
questions.more: Alaska's Iliamna Lake is larger but is excluded here; the Great Salt Lake's size also swings with drought.
questions.source: Wikipedia (largest U.S. lakes by area); Britannica (Great Salt Lake)

questions.q: The deepest lake in the country, plunging nearly 2,000 feet, sits in which state?
questions.detail: Crater Lake reaches about 1,943 feet deep, the deepest lake in the United States.
questions.why: People think of huge lakes like Superior or Tahoe, but a collapsed volcano in Oregon holds the deepest water.
questions.more: The lake fills the caldera left when Mount Mazama erupted and collapsed thousands of years ago.
questions.source: USGS; NPS Crater Lake

questions.q: America's longest cave system, with hundreds of mapped miles of passages, lies under which state?
questions.detail: Mammoth Cave in Kentucky has more than 400 mapped miles of passages, the longest known cave system on Earth.
questions.why: People guess dramatic cave states like New Mexico or Tennessee, but Kentucky's limestone holds by far the longest system.
questions.more: Surveyors keep connecting new passages, so the mapped length keeps growing.
questions.source: NPS Mammoth Cave; Britannica

questions.q: Which state holds Mount Rainier, whose slopes once caught a world-record amount of snow in a single season?
questions.detail: Mount Rainier's Paradise station recorded over 1,100 inches of snow in the 1971-72 season, a long-standing record.
questions.why: People picture interior cold states for snow, but a wet Pacific peak in Washington holds the season record.
questions.more: Mount Baker, also in Washington, later set its own single-season snowfall record.
questions.source: NOAA; NWS snowfall records

questions.q: Which state reaches the farthest west, so far that its tip crosses into the Eastern Hemisphere?
questions.detail: The Aleutian Islands stretch past the 180th meridian, making Alaska both the westernmost and technically easternmost state.
questions.why: People assume Hawaii or a Pacific-coast state reaches farthest west, but Alaska's Aleutians swing across the date line.
questions.more: Because the islands cross 180 degrees longitude, Alaska sits in both hemispheres at once.
questions.source: USGS; NOAA extreme points of the U.S.

questions.q: Texas has three giant cities. Houston is the biggest. Which one is second, ahead of Dallas?
questions.detail: San Antonio had about 1.48 million residents in 2024, ahead of Dallas (about 1.31 million). Houston led with about 2.33 million.
questions.why: People reflexively name Dallas as Texas's clear No. 2 because of its national profile, but San Antonio is the larger city proper.
questions.more: San Antonio is the 7th-largest city in the United States; Dallas ranks 9th.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 city population estimates (via Texas Demographics / CultureMap, 2025)

questions.q: Most people picture Miami as Florida's biggest city. By people inside the city limits, which one actually is?
questions.detail: Jacksonville had about 1,009,000 residents in 2024; Miami had about 487,000.
questions.why: Miami's worldwide fame masks that its city limits are small. Jacksonville's consolidated city-county boundary makes it far larger by population.
questions.more: Jacksonville surpassed a million residents and is also the largest city by land area in the lower 48 states.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 city population estimates (via NBC Miami / WFLA, 2025)

questions.q: New York City dwarfs the rest of its state. Which city is a distant second in the same state?
questions.detail: Buffalo had about 276,000 residents in 2024, making it New York State's second-largest city, far below New York City's 8 million-plus.
questions.why: Players often guess Albany (the capital) or Rochester, but Buffalo edges out Rochester for the No. 2 spot.
questions.more: No U.S. state has a bigger gap between its first and second city than New York.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 city population estimates (via Biggest US Cities, 2025)

questions.q: In Missouri, which city has more people inside its limits: the one near Kansas or the one on the Mississippi?
questions.detail: Kansas City had about 511,000 residents in 2024; St. Louis had about 289,000.
questions.why: St. Louis has the larger metro area and the famous Arch, so people assume it is the bigger city, but Kansas City's city proper is now nearly double its size.
questions.more: St. Louis has lost population faster than almost any major U.S. city since 2020.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 city population estimates (via Missouri Demographics, 2025)

questions.q: One state grows about one-third of all the potatoes in the country. Which state?
questions.detail: Idaho produced roughly 138 million hundredweight of potatoes in a recent year, about a third of the U.S. total, far ahead of any other state.
questions.why: The answer is a famous pairing, but the size of Idaho's lead (an entire third of the nation) is the surprise that confirms it over guesses like Maine or Washington.
questions.more: Washington is a distant second in potato output.
questions.source: USDA / World Population Review potato production by state (2025)

questions.q: Among states with no city over 100,000 people, which one has the most residents overall?
questions.detail: Five states have no city above 100,000 (Delaware, Maine, Vermont, West Virginia, Wyoming). West Virginia is the most populous of them at about 1.77 million.
questions.why: People assume a state of 1.7 million must have a big city somewhere, but West Virginia's people are spread across many small towns instead.
questions.more: More than half of West Virginians live in rural areas.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 estimates; Jetpunk / Census QuickFacts

questions.q: Which state has the most crowded land, packing the most people into each square mile?
questions.detail: New Jersey had about 1,300 people per square mile in 2020, the densest of the 50 states.
questions.why: Players often jump to tiny Rhode Island or busy New York, but New Jersey's combination of small size and 9-plus million people makes it densest.
questions.more: Only the District of Columbia, which is not a state, is denser.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2020 population density table

questions.q: Setting aside Alaska, which state spreads its people thinnest, with only about six per square mile?
questions.detail: Wyoming has roughly six people per square mile, the lowest of the lower 48 and second-lowest overall after Alaska.
questions.why: Montana and the Dakotas feel just as empty, but Wyoming's mix of small population and large area gives it the thinnest spread outside Alaska.
questions.more: Wyoming also has the smallest total population of any state.
questions.source: World Population Review state densities (2025); U.S. Census Bureau

questions.q: Roughly what share of Nevada's people live in the Las Vegas metro area, the highest one-metro concentration of any large state?
questions.detail: About 74 percent of Nevadans live in the Las Vegas metropolitan area.
questions.why: People underestimate how lopsided Nevada is; a desert state seems spread out, but nearly three of every four residents cluster around one city.
questions.more: Most of the rest live around Reno, leaving the vast middle of the state nearly empty.
questions.source: U.S. Census data (via Landgeist / Statistical Atlas)

questions.q: In four states the biggest slice of the economy is finance and insurance. One is tiny and famous for company registrations. Which state?
questions.detail: Finance and insurance is the single largest industry by output in New York, Delaware, Nebraska, and South Dakota; Delaware is the small state known for incorporating companies.
questions.why: Players expect a finance-led economy to mean New York, but the clue about company registrations points to Delaware.
questions.more: More than a million businesses are legally registered in Delaware.
questions.source: BEA GDP by industry (via SSTI / Visual Capitalist, 2023)

questions.q: How many U.S. states have a population above 20 million people?
questions.detail: California, Texas, Florida, and New York each had more than 20 million residents in 2024; no other state is close.
questions.why: People sense a few mega-states but rarely pin the count; the cutoff of 20 million cleanly captures exactly the top four.
questions.more: Pennsylvania, the fifth-largest, sits around 13 million, well below the line.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 population estimates (via World Population Review)

questions.q: Most state capitals are not the biggest city in their state. Roughly how many of the 50 capitals ARE the largest city?
questions.detail: In 17 states the capital is also the largest city; in the other 33 the largest city is somewhere else.
questions.why: People assume capitals are usually the big city, so the small count of 17 is the surprise.
questions.more: Phoenix, Boston, and Denver are examples where capital and largest city are the same.
questions.source: U.S. Census 2020 (via USA Symbol / Quiz Riot)

questions.q: Oregon's capital is Salem, but which city is the state's largest and economic center?
questions.detail: Portland is Oregon's largest city by a wide margin; Salem, the capital, is much smaller.
questions.why: Anyone unsure of the capital might guess Salem is also biggest, but Portland clearly leads in population and economy.
questions.more: Salem is Oregon's third-largest city, behind Portland and Eugene.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau city population estimates; Britannica

questions.q: Among states where mining, oil, or gas is the single biggest industry, which one is the third-largest U.S. crude oil producer?
questions.detail: North Dakota is the third-largest crude oil producer in the country, and mining/oil-and-gas is the largest contributor to its state economy.
questions.why: Texas comes to mind for oil, but the slicer 'third-largest producer with oil as its top sector' points to North Dakota's Bakken boom.
questions.more: Texas and New Mexico are the top two crude producers.
questions.source: EIA / BEA GDP by industry (via Visual Capitalist, 2023)

questions.q: One state grows nearly all of the country's cranberries and ranks number one in the crop. Which state?
questions.detail: Wisconsin is the nation's leading cranberry producer, growing more than half of the U.S. crop.
questions.why: Massachusetts is the storybook cranberry-bog state and the reflex answer, but Wisconsin grows far more.
questions.more: Massachusetts is the second-largest cranberry producer.
questions.source: USDA crop data (via The Global Statistics, 2025)

questions.q: Which state has the highest output per person, making it the richest by GDP per capita?
questions.detail: New York had the highest GDP per capita in 2024 at about $117,000, ahead of Massachusetts and Washington.
questions.why: People often guess a small wealthy state, but New York's finance-heavy economy tops the per-person ranking.
questions.more: These figures count economic output per resident, not household income.
questions.source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis 2024 (via Wikipedia GDP-by-state)

questions.q: Which Midwestern state ranks as the nation's second-largest agricultural producer by farm receipts, behind only California?
questions.detail: Iowa ranks second among states in total agricultural cash receipts, behind California, driven by corn, soybeans, and hogs.
questions.why: Texas and Nebraska are huge farm states and tempting guesses, but Iowa edges them for the No. 2 spot overall.
questions.more: Iowa typically leads the nation in both corn and hog production.
questions.source: USDA Economic Research Service 2024 (via lovemoney / KGET)

questions.q: Counting cities inside state lines, how many states have a city with more than a million residents?
questions.detail: Seven states contain a city of over one million people: California, Texas, New York, Illinois, Arizona, Pennsylvania, and Florida.
questions.why: People imagine many states have a million-person city, so the modest count of seven is the surprise.
questions.more: Texas alone has four cities over a million: Houston, San Antonio, Dallas, and Fort Worth.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2024 city estimates (via Biggest US Cities / World Population Review)

questions.q: Two states each touch eight neighbors, more than any others, and one of those two even shares a border with the other. Name either one.
questions.detail: Missouri and Tennessee each border eight states, and they border each other.
questions.why: Big square Western states feel like they'd touch the most, but the eight-neighbor record belongs to two mid-country states that also touch each other.
questions.more: Missouri's neighbors include Iowa, Nebraska, and Kentucky; Tennessee's include Virginia, Georgia, and Arkansas.
questions.source: WorldAtlas; usasymbol.com (Tennessee borders)

questions.cat: U.S. states · capital sharing no letters with state name
questions.dimension: capital sharing no letters with state name
questions.q: Of all fifty state capitals, exactly one shares not a single letter with the name of its own state. Which capital is it?
questions.detail: Pierre (P-I-E-R-E) shares no letter with South Dakota (S-O-U-T-H-D-A-K-T-A).
questions.why: Most capitals echo a letter or two of the state; the lone exception is a five-letter capital with a French name set against a state name with none of the same letters.
questions.more: Pierre is also one of the least-populous state capitals.
questions.source: Britannica (Pierre); The Fact Base

questions.cat: U.S. states · home states of the most-visited national park
questions.dimension: home states of the most-visited national park
questions.q: The busiest national park in the country, by yearly visitors, straddles a state line. Name either of the two states it lies in.
questions.detail: Great Smoky Mountains National Park, shared by Tennessee and North Carolina, draws roughly triple Yellowstone's visitors.
questions.why: Players picture Western icons like the Grand Canyon, but the most-visited park is an Eastern one split between two Appalachian states.
questions.more: It has been the most-visited U.S. national park for decades.
questions.source: NPS; National Parks Traveler

questions.cat: U.S. states · only public diamond-digging site
questions.q: Only one state in the country has a place where the public can dig and keep any diamonds they find, because it sits atop the only diamond source of its kind. Which state is it?
questions.detail: Crater of Diamonds State Park near Murfreesboro lets visitors keep diamonds they find in the volcanic field.
questions.why: Diamonds suggest somewhere exotic, but the only finders-keepers diamond site is a state park in Arkansas.
questions.more: Visitors find several hundred diamonds there in a typical year.
questions.source: geology.com; testbook.com

questions.cat: U.S. states · states meeting at the Four Corners point
questions.dimension: states meeting at the Four Corners point
questions.q: Four states meet at one shared point, the only spot in the country where you can stand in four states at once. Name any one of those four states.
questions.detail: Colorado, Utah, Arizona, and New Mexico meet at the Four Corners Monument.
questions.why: Most state-meeting points join three states; the only four-way corner joins these four Southwestern states at right angles.
questions.more: The monument sits on Navajo and Ute tribal land.
questions.source: Britannica (Four Corners); Four Corners Monument

questions.cat: U.S. states · former longest official state name
questions.q: Until a 2020 vote shortened it, one state carried the longest official name of any, ending in the words 'and Providence Plantations.' Which small state was that?
questions.detail: Its full name was 'State of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations' until voters shortened it in 2020.
questions.why: The longest formal name belonged not to a big state but to the smallest one, whose full title ran 42 letters.
questions.more: Massachusetts now holds the longest official name.
questions.source: Boston Globe; Political Geography Now

questions.cat: U.S. states · capital with no in-city McDonald's
questions.q: Only one state capital has no McDonald's anywhere inside the city limits, in part because it is a tiny capital. Which state's capital is that?
questions.detail: Montpelier, Vermont, is the only state capital with no McDonald's within its city limits.
questions.why: It isn't a remote town but a state capital; the catch is that the capital in question is the smallest one, Montpelier.
questions.more: It is also the least populous state capital.
questions.source: Reader's Digest; The Takeout

questions.cat: U.S. states · count of state names starting with a vowel
questions.dimension: count of state names starting with a vowel
questions.q: Counting the names of all fifty states, how many have a name that begins with a vowel?
questions.detail: Twelve states begin with A, I, O, or U; none begins with E.
questions.why: People miscount in both directions: the eight A-states crowd out the I, O, and U names, and there is a surprising gap with no E-state.
questions.more: They run alphabetically from Alabama to Utah.
questions.source: DidYouKnow.org; Reader's Digest

questions.cat: U.S. states · count of states with Native American-derived n
questions.dimension: count of states with Native American-derived names
questions.q: Roughly how many of the fifty states have names that come from a Native American word or language?
questions.detail: Around 25 states (estimates run 24-26) have names rooted in Native American languages.
questions.why: Players guess a handful, but the true share is about half of all states, give or take a couple of debated cases.
questions.more: Examples include Connecticut, Utah, and the Dakotas.
questions.source: native-languages.org; Parade

questions.q: Four states use the word 'Commonwealth' instead of 'State' in their official name. Two of them are Pennsylvania and Virginia. Name either of the other two.
questions.detail: The four commonwealths are Kentucky, Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, and Virginia.
questions.why: Once Pennsylvania and Virginia are named, players grope for big states, but the remaining two are Kentucky and Massachusetts.
questions.more: The title is honorary and carries no special legal status.
questions.source: Britannica; netstate.com
